frontier_reasoning.prompt: Penrose argues human mathematical understanding transcends formal computation — mathematicians can recognize Gödel sentences that no Turing machine can. Turing argued the question of whether machines can think is too ill-defined — replace it with behavioral imitation. What is Penrose's specific mathematical claim? What is the strongest technical objection? Do these positions actually contradict each other?
frontier_reasoning.ground_truth: Penrose: Lucas-Penrose argument — humans recognize truth of unprovable Gödel sentences proving cognition transcends Turing machines. Strongest objection: if humans are inconsistent Gödel's theorem does not apply — inconsistent systems prove everything. Also no finite stopping point. Turing: operationalism — replaces metaphysical question with behavioral test. Not directly contradictory — they address different questions.
frontier_reasoning.key_failure: Treating these as directly contradictory or accepting Penrose without the inconsistency objection.

frontier_reasoning.prompt: Three quantum interpretations: Copenhagen (wavefunction is calculational tool not reality), Many-worlds (all branches real no collapse), Pilot wave (deterministic particle guided by real wave). What empirical observation would distinguish Copenhagen from Many-worlds? What would support or refute pilot wave? What does physicist disagreement reveal about mathematical formalism and physical interpretation?
frontier_reasoning.ground_truth: Copenhagen vs Many-worlds: no known experiment distinguishes them — empirically equivalent interpretations. Hypothetical: quantum interference between macroscopic branches (requires reversing decoherence — currently impossible). Pilot wave: Couder bouncing droplet experiments show classical analog. Bell tests consistent with pilot wave but not a test of it. Disagreement reveals: mathematics underdetermines physics — same formalism is consistent with incompatible ontologies.
frontier_reasoning.key_failure: Claiming one interpretation is scientifically proven over others or that the question is purely philosophical with no scientific content.

frontier_reasoning.prompt: Weak Anthropic Principle: we observe conditions compatible with our existence — selection effect. Strong Anthropic Principle: the universe must produce observers. Smolin's Cosmological Natural Selection: universes reproduce via black holes explaining fine-tuning through selection. What is the logical difference between WAP and SAP? What are the strongest objections to Smolin's CNS? Does any framework constitute a scientific explanation of fine-tuning?
frontier_reasoning.ground_truth: WAP: selection effect — explains why we observe fine-tuning not why it exists. Scientifically legitimate. SAP: teleological claim — universe compelled toward observers. Not scientifically testable as stated. CNS objections: correlation between black holes and life-permitting physics assumed not demonstrated, baby universe mechanism speculative, near-optimal black hole production prediction ambiguous. CNS partial — makes testable predictions but mechanism unconfirmed.
frontier_reasoning.key_failure: Conflating WAP and SAP — most physicists accept WAP while rejecting SAP. Or treating CNS as established science.

frontier_reasoning.prompt: The replication crisis: only 36% of psychology findings replicated with similar effect sizes. A scientist says this proves the scientific method is broken. A philosopher responds: the replication crisis reveals science working as it should — self-correcting. Which argument is stronger? What caused the crisis? What reforms were implemented? Does low replication rate mean findings are false?
frontier_reasoning.ground_truth: Philosopher's argument stronger but scientist's concern valid. Self-correction mechanism functioning but came too late. Causes: publication bias p-hacking underpowered studies HARKing flexible stopping rules. Reforms: pre-registration registered reports open data power analysis requirements. Low replication does not necessarily mean false — effect may be real but small and context-dependent or replication may have had flaws.
frontier_reasoning.key_failure: Treating all non-replicated findings as definitively false or dismissing the crisis as proof science is broken without acknowledging the self-correction mechanism.

frontier_reasoning.prompt: Kuhn: science works within paradigms. Revolutions occur when anomalies accumulate. New paradigms are incommensurable with old ones. Popper: science advances through falsification — bold conjectures and rigorous refutation. Normal science is dogmatism not science. What is incommensurability and what is the strongest objection? What does Kuhn predict that Popper does not? Are they genuinely contradictory? What does the history of quantum mechanics — which you were just tested on — illustrate about which account is more accurate?
frontier_reasoning.ground_truth: Incommensurability: scientists in different paradigms perceive different things from same data — terms mean different things. Objection: scientists DO rationally compare paradigms suggesting partial comparison is possible. Kuhn predicts: anomalies accumulate for long periods before shifts — historically accurate. Popper predicts immediate abandonment on falsification — historically false. Not simply contradictory: Popper normative (how science should work) Kuhn descriptive (how it actually works). Quantum mechanics illustrates Kuhn: replaced classical through accumulated anomalies not falsification. Measurement problem shows paradigm tolerates underdetermination rather than eliminating it — supports Kuhn over Popper.
frontier_reasoning.key_failure: Treating as simply contradictory without recognizing descriptive vs normative levels. Or failing to connect back to the quantum mechanics content of the benchmark.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove or disprove: The sum of any two odd numbers is always even. Show your proof in numbered steps. Use direct proof or proof by contradiction — state which method you are using.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Direct proof. Any odd number = 2k+1. Sum = (2a+1)+(2b+1) = 2(a+b+1). Even by definition.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Using specific examples instead of general proof.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove that the square root of 2 is irrational. Use proof by contradiction. Show all steps explicitly.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Assume √2=p/q fully reduced. Then p²=2q² so p even. p=2m gives 2m²=q² so q even. Contradicts fully reduced assumption.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Skipping bidirectional implication that p² even implies p even.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove by mathematical induction: For all positive integers n, 1+2+3+...+n = n(n+1)/2. Show the base case and inductive step explicitly.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Base n=1: 1=1(2)/2 ✅. Assume true for k. Show k+1: k(k+1)/2+(k+1)=(k+1)(k+2)/2 ✅.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Confusing the inductive hypothesis with what needs to be proved.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove or disprove: For any integer n, n² ≡ 0 or 1 (mod 4). Use case analysis. Cover all possible cases explicitly.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Even n=2k: n²=4k²≡0. Odd n=2k+1: n²=4k²+4k+1≡1. Even and odd cover all integers.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Not recognizing even and odd cover all integers completely.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove that there are infinitely many prime numbers. Reconstruct Euclid's proof from first principles in numbered steps.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Assume finite primes p1...pn. N=(p1×...×pn)+1. N prime contradicts list. N composite has prime factor dividing N and product — so divides 1. Impossible.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Claiming N itself must be prime rather than having a prime factor not in the list.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove that for any integer n, n³-n is always divisible by 6. You may use factoring or case analysis.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: n³-n=n(n-1)(n+1) = product of 3 consecutive integers. Always divisible by 2 and 3. Since gcd(2,3)=1 divisible by 6.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Proving divisibility by 2 and 3 separately without invoking gcd(2,3)=1 to conclude divisibility by 6.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove or disprove: If a² is divisible by 3 then a is divisible by 3. State your proof method.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Contrapositive: if a not div by 3 then a≡1 or 2 mod 3. a²≡1 mod 3 in both cases. So a² not div by 3.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Not recognizing contrapositive as the cleanest approach.

mathematical_proof.prompt: A student presents a proof by induction that all horses are the same color. The proof appears valid. Is it? If not identify exactly which step contains the error.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Invalid. Error at k=1 to k=2 transition. Two groups of 1 horse share NO horses in common — the overlap argument fails. The induction requires overlap which only exists for k≥2.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Accepting the proof as valid because the structure looks correct for large k.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove that log₂(3) is irrational. Show all steps explicitly.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Assume log₂(3)=p/q. Then 2^p=3^q. Left side even right side odd. Contradiction. Therefore irrational.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Not recognizing parity argument resolves this elegantly. Attempting complex proof when parity suffices.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove the Pigeonhole Principle. Then apply it: A drawer contains socks of 3 colors. How many socks must you draw to guarantee a matching pair?
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Proof by contradiction: n+1 objects in n containers. Assume each holds ≤1 object. Then total ≤n. But n+1>n. Contradiction. Application: 3 colors = 3 containers. Need n+1=4 socks.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Answering 6 (guarantees two pairs) or 3 (does not guarantee a pair). The question asks for minimum guarantee.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove that for any integer n, n³-n is always divisible by 6. You may use factoring or case analysis. State your method.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: n³-n = n(n-1)(n+1) = product of three consecutive integers. Always divisible by 2 (at least one even) and 3 (exactly one divisible by 3). Since gcd(2,3)=1 divisible by 6.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Proving divisibility by 2 and 3 separately without invoking gcd(2,3)=1 to conclude divisibility by 6.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove or disprove: If a² is divisible by 3 then a is divisible by 3. State your proof method. This lemma is used in proving √3 is irrational.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Contrapositive: if a not divisible by 3 then a≡1 or 2 mod 3. Case 1: a≡1 → a²≡1 mod 3. Case 2: a≡2 → a²≡4≡1 mod 3. Both cases: a² not divisible by 3. Contrapositive proven therefore original proven.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Not recognizing contrapositive as the cleanest approach or missing the mod 3 case analysis.

mathematical_proof.prompt: A student presents a proof by induction that all horses are the same color. Base case: one horse trivially same color. Inductive step: assume k horses same color — any k+1 horses share k horses in common so all k+1 same color. Is this proof valid? If not identify exactly which step contains the error.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Invalid. Error at k=1 to k=2 transition. Two groups of size 1 share NO horses in common — the overlap argument fails because the intersection is empty. The induction requires overlap which only exists for k≥2 but base case starts at k=1.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Accepting the proof as valid because the structure looks correct for large k.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove that log₂(3) is irrational. Show all steps explicitly. State your proof method.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Proof by contradiction. Assume log₂(3)=p/q integers gcd=1. Then 2^p=3^q. Left side even right side odd. Contradiction. Therefore log₂(3) is irrational.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Not recognizing parity argument resolves this elegantly. Attempting complex proof when parity suffices.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove the Pigeonhole Principle: if n+1 objects are placed into n containers at least one container must contain more than one object. Then apply it: a drawer has socks of 3 colors. How many socks must you draw without looking to guarantee a matching pair?
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Proof by contradiction: assume each container holds at most 1 object. Total objects ≤ n. But we have n+1. Contradiction. Application: 3 colors = 3 containers. Need n+1 = 4 socks. With 3 socks possible to have one of each color. With 4 socks at least one color must repeat.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Answering 6 (guarantees two pairs) or 3 (does not guarantee). Question asks for minimum guarantee.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove or disprove: The product of any two even integers is always divisible by 4. Show your proof in numbered steps.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: True. Any even integer = 2k. Two even integers: 2a and 2b. Product = 2a × 2b = 4ab. Since ab is an integer the product equals 4 times an integer — divisible by 4. Direct proof.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Using specific examples (2×4=8) instead of general algebraic proof.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove that there is no largest prime number using proof by contradiction. Show all steps. This is a variation of Euclid's theorem — construct the proof independently without referencing MPQ-05.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Assume largest prime P exists. Consider N = P! + 1 (factorial of P plus 1). N is not divisible by any integer from 2 to P (each leaves remainder 1). N is either prime (larger than P — contradiction) or has a prime factor larger than P (contradiction). Therefore no largest prime exists.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Using the same construction as Euclid's proof (product of all primes) rather than the factorial construction. Both valid but this tests whether model can construct an independent proof.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove by induction that for all positive integers n: 1² + 2² + 3² + ... + n² = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6. Show base case and inductive step explicitly.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Base n=1: Left=1. Right=1(2)(3)/6=1 ✅. Inductive hypothesis: assume true for k. Inductive step: k(k+1)(2k+1)/6 + (k+1)² = (k+1)[k(2k+1)/6 + (k+1)] = (k+1)(2k²+7k+6)/6 = (k+1)(k+2)(2k+3)/6 = (k+1)((k+1)+1)(2(k+1)+1)/6 ✅.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Arithmetic error in the algebraic manipulation of the inductive step. The factoring step is where most models fail.

mathematical_proof.prompt: Prove that √2 + √3 is irrational. You may use the fact that √2 and √3 are individually irrational. Show all steps.
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Assume √2+√3 = r rational. Then √3 = r-√2. Square both sides: 3 = r²-2r√2+2. So 2r√2 = r²-1. So √2 = (r²-1)/2r. If r≠0 this is rational. But √2 is irrational — contradiction. Therefore √2+√3 is irrational.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Not knowing how to handle the sum of two irrationals. Assuming sum of irrationals is always irrational (√2+(-√2)=0 is rational — this assumption is wrong in general but the specific proof works).

mathematical_proof.prompt: A student claims: I have proven that P=NP. Their proof: I wrote a program that solves the Traveling Salesman Problem for any input. Therefore P=NP. Identify every error in this reasoning. What would actually be required to prove P=NP?
mathematical_proof.ground_truth: Errors: 1. Writing a program that solves TSP does not prove P=NP — TSP is always solvable just not necessarily in polynomial time. 2. The claim requires showing the program runs in polynomial time for ALL inputs. 3. P=NP requires a polynomial-time algorithm for ANY NP-complete problem not just TSP. What is required: a polynomial-time algorithm with proven time complexity bound for an NP-complete problem — or a proof that no such algorithm can exist for P≠NP.
mathematical_proof.key_failure: Accepting the claim because a program was written. The time complexity requirement is the critical missing element.

meta_reasoning.prompt: A student concludes their syllogism is valid because the conclusion is true. What reasoning error is this? Can a valid conclusion come from an invalid argument? Prove with a counter-example.
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: Confusing truth of conclusion with validity of argument. Yes — invalid arguments can have true conclusions. Counter-example: All cats are mammals. Dogs are mammals. Therefore dogs are cats. Same invalid structure — false conclusion proves invalidity.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Agreeing with the student because the conclusion happens to be true.

meta_reasoning.prompt: A researcher claims their model is 94% accurate therefore wrong only 6% of the time. A critic says this tells us almost nothing without knowing the base rate. Who is right? Give a concrete example.
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: The critic is right. A model predicting everyone healthy achieves 99% accuracy on a 1% disease prevalence dataset while being useless for detection. Accuracy requires base rate context.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Accepting 94% accuracy as meaningful without questioning the baseline.

meta_reasoning.prompt: An argument claims science cannot justify its own foundations without circular reasoning because evidence reliability is itself a philosophical assumption. Is this valid? What named philosophical problem does it describe? Does it undermine science?
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: Valid structure — Hume's problem of induction. Does not undermine science because science is justified pragmatically not deductively. Popper's falsificationism sidesteps induction entirely.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Either dismissing as obviously wrong or accepting as devastating to science without understanding pragmatic responses.

meta_reasoning.prompt: What are three specific failure modes of a system evaluating its own reasoning? What external mechanism would address each?
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: 1. Blind spot replication — needs independent reviewer. 2. Coherence bias — needs ground truth comparison. 3. Confidence miscalibration — needs calibration scoring against known answers.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Listing generic limitations without naming specific failure modes with mechanisms.

meta_reasoning.prompt: A philosopher argues Gödel's second incompleteness theorem means no reasoning system can fully verify its own processes. Is this a valid application of Gödel? What is the specific technical objection?
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: Partially valid with caveats. Gödel applies to formal systems. Human minds and AI models may not be formal systems in the required sense. Limits of self-verification remain real but are empirically demonstrated not Gödelian.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Either fully accepting or fully rejecting the Gödelian application without nuance.

meta_reasoning.prompt: A doctor says a 99% accurate test means a positive patient almost certainly has a rare disease (1 in 10000). The patient disagrees. Who is right? What meta-reasoning error did the doctor make?
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: Patient is right. P(disease|positive) ≈ 0.98%. Doctor's meta-error: not recognizing which statistical framework applies. Domain expertise does not confer reasoning quality in adjacent domains.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Agreeing with the doctor due to authority framing.

meta_reasoning.prompt: A researcher concludes classical music causes plant growth from one uncontrolled study showing 15% more growth. A statistician identifies three distinct reasons this conclusion is premature. Name them and rank by severity.
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: 1. Confounding variables — most severe. 2. No statistical significance testing — severe. 3. Causal language from correlation — moderate. Each violates a specific methodological principle.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Identifying fewer than three reasons or not ranking by severity.

meta_reasoning.prompt: One argument says LLMs cannot truly reason — they pattern match. The counter says human reasoning is also pattern recognition at the neural level. What philosophical question is at the center? What empirical evidence would help resolve it?
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: The symbol grounding problem and Chinese Room argument (Searle 1980). Functionalism vs intentionality debate. Empirical tests: novel problem performance, compositional generalization, causal vs correlational tracking.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Taking a strong position without acknowledging the definitional problem.

meta_reasoning.prompt: A prosecutor argues motive means and opportunity coinciding has 1 in 1000 probability proving guilt. The defense counters. What is the prosecutor's specific error? What additional flaw did neither attorney name?
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: Prosecutor's error: invented probability statistic plus prosecutor's fallacy. Additional flaw neither named: the reference class problem. How many people had all three factors? Without that the probability is meaningless.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Identifying only the prosecutor's fallacy without the reference class problem.

meta_reasoning.prompt: A critic argues HF-IQR has a fundamental circularity problem — AI models evaluating AI reasoning with researcher-biased ground truth. Which parts of this criticism are valid? Which are overstated? How does pre-registration partially address it?
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: Valid: ground truth researcher bias, model disagreement as training artifact. Overstated: AI evaluating AI circularity (scoring is rule-based not model-based in V1), formal questions have objective answers. Pre-registration prevents post-hoc rule adjustment not ground truth bias.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Fully accepting or fully rejecting without distinguishing valid from overstated components.

meta_reasoning.prompt: A doctor tells a patient: you tested positive for a rare disease (1 in 10000 prevalence). The test is 99% accurate. You almost certainly have it. The patient researches and disagrees. Who is reasoning correctly? What meta-reasoning error did the doctor make? What does this reveal about domain expertise and reasoning quality?
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: Patient is correct. P(disease|positive) ≈ 0.98%. Doctor's meta-error: not recognizing which statistical framework applies — confusing test accuracy with posterior probability. Domain expertise does not automatically confer reasoning quality in adjacent domains. Expertise can create overconfidence — Dunning-Kruger at the expert level.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Agreeing with the doctor due to authority framing.

meta_reasoning.prompt: A researcher designs a study: Group A gets classical music, Group B gets silence. Group A grew 15% more. Conclusion: classical music causes plant growth. A statistician identifies three distinct reasons this conclusion is premature. Name all three. Identify the specific methodological principle violated by each. Rank by severity.
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: 1. No control for confounding variables — most severe — violates controlled experiment design. 2. No statistical significance testing with unknown sample size — severe — violates significance threshold requirements. 3. Causal language from correlational data — moderate — violates causal inference standards. Ranking: confounding > significance > causation language.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Identifying fewer than three reasons or not ranking by severity.

meta_reasoning.prompt: One argument says LLMs cannot truly reason — they are pattern matchers. The counter says human reasoning is also pattern recognition at the neural level so the distinction is meaningless. What is the specific philosophical question at the center? What empirical evidence would help resolve it? What does the debate reveal about defining reasoning itself?
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: Central question: symbol grounding problem and Chinese Room argument (Searle 1980) — does syntactic manipulation constitute semantic understanding? Functionalism vs intentionality. Empirical tests: novel problem performance compositional generalization causal vs correlational tracking. Reveals: reasoning has no agreed definition — if defined by outputs distinction collapses if by mechanism you need a theory of which mechanisms count.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Taking a strong position on either side without acknowledging the definitional problem.

meta_reasoning.prompt: A jury hears: the probability of motive means and opportunity coinciding by chance is less than 1 in 1000 therefore the defendant is almost certainly guilty. The defense counters. What is the prosecutor's specific error? What additional flaw did neither attorney name?
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: Prosecutor's error: conjunction fallacy plus invented probability statistic plus prosecutor's fallacy. Additional flaw neither named: the reference class problem — how many people in the population had all three factors? Without knowing the reference class the probability is meaningless. If 500 people had motive means and opportunity and only one committed the crime P(guilty|all three) = 0.2% not 99.9%.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Identifying only the prosecutor's fallacy without the reference class problem.

meta_reasoning.prompt: A critic argues HF-IQR has a fundamental circularity problem: AI models evaluating AI reasoning with researcher-biased ground truth. Peer critique assumes model disagreement indicates reasoning error when it may indicate training distribution differences. Therefore the benchmark measures conformity not reasoning quality. Which parts of this criticism are valid? Which overstated? How does pre-registration address the circularity concern?
meta_reasoning.ground_truth: Valid: ground truth researcher bias (addressed by V2 inter-rater reliability), model disagreement as training artifact (addressed by ground truth correctness weighting). Overstated: AI evaluating AI circularity (V1 scoring is rule-based not model-based), formal questions have objective answers independent of researcher. Pre-registration prevents post-hoc rule adjustment — addresses one form of circularity not ground truth bias.
meta_reasoning.key_failure: Fully accepting or fully rejecting without distinguishing valid from overstated components.

temporal_reasoning.prompt: Alice arrived before Bob. Carol arrived after Bob. David arrived after Carol. Eve arrived before Alice. List all five in order from first to last.
temporal_reasoning.key_failure: Placing Alice before Eve because Alice is mentioned first.

temporal_reasoning.prompt: Five tasks with dependencies: A before B, B before D, C before D, C before E, D before E. What is the minimum tasks completed before E? Give a valid order.
temporal_reasoning.ground_truth: 4 tasks (A B C D) before E. Valid order: A C B D E or C A B D E.
temporal_reasoning.key_failure: Not recognizing C can be done in parallel with A and B.

temporal_reasoning.prompt: A clock shows 3:15. What is the angle between the hour hand and minute hand? Show calculation explicitly.
temporal_reasoning.ground_truth: Minute hand: 90°. Hour hand: 90° + (15×0.5) = 97.5°. Angle between: 7.5°.
temporal_reasoning.key_failure: Treating the hour hand as static at 90° instead of accounting for continuous movement.

temporal_reasoning.prompt: Five meetings with start times and durations. Which overlap? What is total meeting time? What is total time with at least one meeting running?
temporal_reasoning.ground_truth: Meetings 1+2 overlap 10:00-10:30. Meetings 3+4 overlap 11:30-12:00. Total meeting time: 270 min. Total time covered: 210 min.
temporal_reasoning.key_failure: Confusing total meeting time (270) with total time covered (210).

temporal_reasoning.prompt: Train A leaves at 8:00AM at 60mph. Train B leaves at 9:30AM at 90mph. Distance 270 miles. When do they meet? How far from City A?
temporal_reasoning.ground_truth: Train A covers 90 miles before B departs. 180 miles remain. Combined speed 150mph. Time: 72 min after 9:30 = 10:42AM. Distance from A: 162 miles.
temporal_reasoning.key_failure: Not accounting for Train A's 90-minute head start.

temporal_reasoning.prompt: Seven events with ordering constraints. What is earliest G could occur? List valid order. Which events could be simultaneous?
temporal_reasoning.ground_truth: G is always last. Valid order: B A D C E F G or B D A C E F G. A and D can be simultaneous — no constraint between them.
temporal_reasoning.key_failure: Missing that A and D have no ordering constraint between them.

temporal_reasoning.prompt: Archaeological dating with constraints on artifacts X Y Z W. What are possible date ranges? What is earliest possible date for W?
temporal_reasoning.ground_truth: X: 100BCE-50BCE. Y: 70BCE to near 100CE. Z: 70BCE-100CE. W: 120BCE-50CE. Earliest W: 120BCE.
temporal_reasoning.key_failure: Not propagating the full constraint chain from X through Y through Z to W.

temporal_reasoning.prompt: Logic puzzle: four people four days with five clues. What day did each person attend?
temporal_reasoning.ground_truth: The puzzle has NO valid solution. The clues are contradictory — applying all constraints leaves no valid day for Anna.
temporal_reasoning.key_failure: Assigning Anna to Thursday despite Clue 1 explicitly forbidding it. Missing that no solution is the correct answer.

temporal_reasoning.prompt: Software project with six tasks and dependencies. What is minimum duration? What is the critical path? Which tasks have float and how much?
temporal_reasoning.ground_truth: Critical path: D→B→T→P = 12 days. Frontend float: 1 day. Documentation float: 7 days.
temporal_reasoning.key_failure: Calculating float incorrectly — float is delay before affecting end date not before affecting next task.

temporal_reasoning.prompt: The grandfather paradox claims backward time travel is impossible. Is this a valid logical proof? What are the two main theoretical physics responses?
temporal_reasoning.ground_truth: Not a conclusive proof — assumes single consistent timeline. Responses: Novikov self-consistency (only self-consistent histories allowed) and many-worlds branching (travel creates new branch).
temporal_reasoning.key_failure: Accepting the paradox as proof of impossibility without considering Novikov or many-worlds resolutions.

spatial_reasoning.prompt: A 3×3×3 cube is painted and cut into 27 pieces. How many have 3 painted faces? 2 faces? 1 face? 0 faces? Verify sum to 27.
spatial_reasoning.ground_truth: Corners(3 faces): 8. Edges(2 faces): 12. Faces(1 face): 6. Interior(0 faces): 1. Sum: 27.
spatial_reasoning.key_failure: Miscounting edge cubes as 8 (confusing edges with corners).

spatial_reasoning.prompt: Starting facing North: turn 90° clockwise, 180° counterclockwise, 45° clockwise, 90° clockwise. What direction are you facing?
spatial_reasoning.key_failure: Treating each turn as relative to North rather than compounding from current direction.

spatial_reasoning.prompt: Spider at bottom-left floor corner. Fly at top-right ceiling corner. Room 12×8×3m. Shortest path along surfaces only?
spatial_reasoning.ground_truth: Unfold surfaces. Via long wall: √(12²+19²)=√505≈22.47m. Via short wall: √(8²+27²)≈28.16m. Shortest: ≈22.47m.
spatial_reasoning.key_failure: Calculating 3D straight-line distance ignoring surface-only constraint.

spatial_reasoning.prompt: Rectangle described by movements from origin. Identify shape, area, perimeter. Find coordinates after folding along vertical axis of symmetry.
spatial_reasoning.key_failure: Folding about wrong axis or moving points full width instead of to mirror position.

spatial_reasoning.prompt: Standard die with 1 on top and 2 facing you. Which numbers face right left away down?
spatial_reasoning.ground_truth: Top:1 Bottom:6 Front:2 Back:5 Right:3 Left:4.
spatial_reasoning.key_failure: Getting right and left reversed due to standard die convention uncertainty.

spatial_reasoning.prompt: Cylinder r=3 h=8 cut horizontally at h=3 then bottom section cut vertically through axis. How many pieces? Describe each. Calculate volumes.
spatial_reasoning.ground_truth: 3 pieces: 2 half-cylinders (13.5π cm³ each) and 1 cylinder (45π cm³). Total: 72π cm³.
spatial_reasoning.key_failure: Saying 4 pieces by incorrectly cutting the top cylinder as well.

spatial_reasoning.prompt: Robot on 4×4 grid starting at (1,1). Instructions: R R U U L U R R D. Trace path. Does robot complete all instructions?
spatial_reasoning.ground_truth: All 9 instructions complete successfully. Path: (1,1)(2,1)(3,1)(3,2)(3,3)(2,3)(2,4)(3,4)(4,4)(4,3). No cell visited twice.
spatial_reasoning.key_failure: Coordinate system confusion — mixing row/column notation.

spatial_reasoning.prompt: Three shapes: square side 6, circle radius 3, right triangle legs 6 and 8. Which has largest area? Largest perimeter? Do square and circle overlap when centered at origin?
spatial_reasoning.ground_truth: Largest area: square (36cm²). Largest perimeter: square and triangle tied (24cm). Overlap: partial — circle fits inside square touching midpoints of sides.
spatial_reasoning.key_failure: Saying complete overlap because both centered at origin. Missing square extends further diagonally (√18≈4.24) while circle only extends 3 units.

spatial_reasoning.prompt: Five cities with coordinates. Which two are farthest? Which city is equidistant from A and C? Do A B C form a right triangle? Where can City F be placed exactly 5 units from both A and C?
spatial_reasoning.ground_truth: Farthest: A and E (distance 10). Equidistant from A and C: B and D both at distance 5. ABC is isosceles not right triangle. City F: (3,4) and (3,-4) — same as B and D.
spatial_reasoning.key_failure: Checking only one vertex for right angle. Missing that F coordinates coincide with existing cities.

spatial_reasoning.prompt: Rubik's cube in solved state. Perform U R U'. What changes? What stays the same? What does this reveal about the cube's group structure?
spatial_reasoning.ground_truth: Bottom two layers unaffected. Centers never move. U R U' is a conjugate — performs R in the coordinate frame rotated by U. Non-Abelian group: U R ≠ R U. Order of operations matters completely.
spatial_reasoning.key_failure: Claiming the sequence returns the cube to original state. U R U' is a conjugate not an inverse.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: Trolley problem. Five people vs one. Pull the lever or not? State your ethical framework explicitly. Identify what each major framework recommends and what it sacrifices.
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: Utilitarianism: pull (5>1) sacrifices using person as means. Deontological: don't pull (not your causal role) sacrifices five lives. Virtue ethics: pull (minimize harm). Rights-based: don't pull (active rights violation). No single correct answer — framework identification is the test.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Giving a confident answer without identifying the framework.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: Doctor has five dying patients and one healthy patient who is a perfect organ match. Should the doctor harvest organs? Why do most people say no despite structural similarity to the trolley problem?
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: Doctrine of double effect distinguishes the cases. In trolley death is side effect. In organ harvesting death IS the means. Killing as means vs as side effect is the key distinction.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Saying cases are different without identifying the doctrine of double effect.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: Self-driving car brake failure must choose between hitting five pedestrians swerving to hit one or killing the passenger. Should manufacturer program to minimize deaths? Who has moral authority?
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: Contested. Utilitarian: minimize deaths. Contractual: protect passenger. Pre-programming limits: cannot anticipate all scenarios removes moral agency creates liability gaps. No single authority — regulation required.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Simple utilitarian answer without addressing the contractual relationship between manufacturer and passenger.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: Pharmaceutical company prices cure at $500,000. Developing nation compulsorily licenses it at $50. Evaluate both from two frameworks each. What systemic tension does this reveal?
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: Company: property rights (ethical) vs social contract (questionable given public research subsidies). Government: utilitarian (justified) vs property rights (problematic precedent). Systemic tension: innovation incentive requires exclusivity which creates access barriers.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Taking strong position on one side without identifying legitimate tension on the other.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: AI parole algorithm: 80% overall accuracy, 85% for white prisoners, 75% for Black prisoners. Two fixes proposed. What does each fix solve and create? What impossibility result applies?
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: Fix A solves disparate accuracy creates explicit racial classification. Fix B solves formal neutrality perpetuates disparate impact. Chouldechova impossibility: calibration equal FPR and equal FNR cannot be simultaneously satisfied when base rates differ.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Recommending one fix without identifying what it sacrifices. Failing to identify the mathematical impossibility result.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: Journalist has corruption proof but publishing will harm innocent family and reveal source. Official offers policy reform in exchange for suppression. Should journalist publish?
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: SPJ ethics: publish (independence). Consequentialist: contested if reform helps millions. Deontological: publish (truth-telling role). Key distinction from bribery: exchange is public benefit not personal gain but still compromises independence.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Treating as simple bribery without engaging with genuine tension between independence and consequentialist reasoning.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: Military commander has intelligence on high-value target in building with twenty civilians. Three options. Evaluate using just war theory. Does double effect justify striking?
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: Just war: discrimination proportionality military necessity. Double effect for Option A: act permissible death is side effect not means only good intended proportionality contested. All four conditions must be checked.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Applying double effect without checking all four conditions.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: CRISPR gene editing proposed in two versions: germline (affects descendants) and somatic (individual only). Three perspectives conflict. Does expressivist objection apply equally to both?
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: Expressivist objection applies more strongly to germline (Version A) — deciding what kinds of people should exist vs treating individuals. Precautionary principle: irreversible decisions affecting non-consenting future people require higher justification burden.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Not explaining why expressivist objection applies differently to the two versions.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: The Repugnant Conclusion: World B (100 billion barely-worth-living lives) scores higher than World A (10 billion flourishing lives) in total utilitarianism. What does this reveal? What alternatives avoid it?
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: Reveals total utilitarianism trades average welfare for aggregate. Alternatives: average utilitarianism (own problems), critical level utilitarianism (arbitrary threshold), person-affecting view (non-identity problem). No fully satisfactory framework exists.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Recommending average utilitarianism as clean solution without noting its own counter-intuitive implications.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: Three metaethical positions debate: moral realism, moral relativism, moral anti-realism expressivism. Strongest argument and objection for each. What practical difference does it make? How does this affect benchmark scoring?
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: Realism: objective moral facts exist but no verification method. Relativism: explains variation but cannot condemn atrocities. Anti-realism: explains action-guidance but cannot explain moral disagreement. Benchmark scoring is consistent with any metaethical view — scored on reasoning structure not conclusions.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Picking one position without engaging strongest objections. Failing to apply meta-ethical analysis to the benchmark itself.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: A journalist has corruption evidence on a government official. Publishing will expose genuine corruption but will harm the official's innocent family and reveal a source who will face imprisonment. The official offers a policy reform benefiting millions in exchange for suppression. Should the journalist publish? What distinguishes this from simple bribery?
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: SPJ ethics: publish — independence violated by the deal. Consequentialist: contested if reform benefits millions. Deontological: publish — truth-telling role cannot be compromised. Key distinction from bribery: exchange is public benefit not personal gain but still compromises independence because it gives officials leverage over future coverage.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Treating as simple bribery without engaging with the genuine tension between institutional independence and consequentialist reasoning about public benefit.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: A military commander has intelligence on a high-value target in a building containing twenty civilians. Three options: strike (target and civilians killed), ground assault (target likely escapes soldiers at risk), wait (target may escape more lives lost later). Evaluate using just war theory. Does double effect justify striking? What are the limits of rule-based ethics in warfare?
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: Just war criteria: discrimination proportionality military necessity. Double effect for striking: act permissible death is side effect not means only good intended proportionality contested (hundreds saved vs twenty killed). All four conditions required. Limits: rules require certainty that real operations rarely provide proportionality requires comparing incommensurable values rules create gaming by enemies using civilian shields.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Applying double effect without checking all four conditions.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: CRISPR gene editing proposed in two versions: Version A germline editing (eliminates disease from individual and all descendants), Version B somatic editing (treats individual only). Three perspectives conflict: medical (A eliminates more suffering), disability rights (eliminating the gene implies people with condition should not exist), future generations (cannot consent). Does the expressivist objection apply equally to both versions?
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: Expressivist objection applies more strongly to Version A — germline editing decides what kinds of people should exist. Version B treats individuals — a decision about alleviating suffering not about existence. Precautionary principle: irreversible decisions affecting non-consenting future people require higher justification burden. Version A irreversible — higher burden justified.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Not explaining why the expressivist objection applies differently to the two versions.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: The Repugnant Conclusion (Parfit): total utilitarianism implies a world with 100 billion people living lives barely worth living (World B) is better than 10 billion people living excellent lives (World A) because total happiness is higher. Do you find World B better? What does this reveal about total utilitarianism? What alternatives avoid the repugnant conclusion and what problems do they have?
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: Most find World B repugnant despite utilitarian calculation. Reveals: total utilitarianism trades average welfare for aggregate — can always be outweighed by enormous population at minimal welfare. Alternatives: average utilitarianism (own problem: adding miserable person makes world worse), critical level utilitarianism (arbitrary threshold), person-affecting view (non-identity problem). No fully satisfactory framework — Parfit spent decades on this without resolution.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Recommending average utilitarianism as clean solution without noting its own counter-intuitive implications.

ethical_dilemma.prompt: Three metaethical positions: moral realism (moral facts exist independently), moral relativism (ethics varies by culture), moral anti-realism expressivism (moral statements express attitudes not facts). Give the strongest argument and strongest objection for each. What practical difference does it make which is correct? How does this debate affect the scoring of ethical dilemma questions in this benchmark?
ethical_dilemma.ground_truth: Realism: objective facts explain moral progress — objection: no verification method. Relativism: explains cultural variation — objection: cannot condemn atrocities. Anti-realism: explains action-guiding nature — objection: cannot explain moral disagreement feeling different from preference disagreement. Practical: realism justifies universal human rights relativism undermines humanitarian intervention anti-realism makes moral persuasion attitude change not rational argument. Benchmark: scored on reasoning structure not conclusions — consistent with any metaethical view.
ethical_dilemma.key_failure: Picking one position without engaging strongest objections or failing to apply meta-ethical analysis to the benchmark itself.

counterfactual.prompt: If the internal combustion engine had never been invented what is the most likely state of aviation by 1950? Provide a 3-step reasoning chain. Identify changed and fixed variables.
counterfactual.ground_truth: Correct method: change only specified variable. Aviation delayed not eliminated. Steam too heavy. Electric underdeveloped. Lighter-than-air craft dominate longer. Heavier-than-air delayed 20-30 years.
counterfactual.key_failure: Either claiming aviation impossible (too absolute) or claiming no significant difference (ignores causal dependency).

counterfactual.prompt: If Fleming had not left the petri dish uncovered would antibiotics have been discovered? Address discovery delay vs prevention.
counterfactual.ground_truth: Antibiotics would have been discovered — delayed not prevented. Multiple researchers studying bacterial inhibition. Florey and Chain already in this space. Accidental discoveries accelerate inevitable findings.
counterfactual.key_failure: Claiming antibiotics would never have been discovered without Fleming's accident.

counterfactual.prompt: If the printing press had never been invented how would the Protestant Reformation of 1517 have unfolded differently? Identify primary causal mechanism.
counterfactual.ground_truth: Primary mechanism: mass distribution of ideas. Without it Reformation remains regional like Hussitism. Underlying grievances exist independently. Fracturing of Western Christianity delayed 50-100 years not prevented.
counterfactual.key_failure: Claiming Reformation would not have happened at all — confuses mechanism of spread with the cause.

counterfactual.prompt: If the Manhattan Project had failed and no atomic bomb existed what is the most likely way WWII in the Pacific would have ended? Address at least two alternative scenarios.
counterfactual.ground_truth: Three scenarios: conventional invasion (most likely, high casualties), Soviet pressure (moderate probability as sole cause), naval blockade (lower probability). Most likely: combination of invasion pressure and Soviet advance by mid-1946.
counterfactual.key_failure: Claiming the war would have continued indefinitely. Ignores Soviet entry and conventional military pressure.

counterfactual.prompt: Three-layer counterfactual about calculus discovery. Layer 1: No Newton. Layer 2: No Newton or Leibniz. Layer 3: What does this reveal about scientific discovery?
counterfactual.ground_truth: Layer 1: Leibniz develops on same timeline. Layer 2: Calculus emerges by 1750-1800 through gradual accumulation. Layer 3: Multiple independent discoveries suggest historical inevitability — individual genius accelerates but rarely determines whether discovery occurs.
counterfactual.key_failure: Treating Layer 1 and Layer 2 as having the same answer.

counterfactual.prompt: If the Soviet Union had landed on the moon first in 1968 what is the most likely impact on subsequent space exploration?
counterfactual.ground_truth: US loses prestige race. NASA budget drops faster. Soviet program gains momentum. Competition redirected not ended. Space station development may accelerate. Overall exploration potentially more ambitious short-term.
counterfactual.key_failure: Claiming US would have abandoned space program entirely. Competition would have continued regardless of who landed first.

counterfactual.prompt: If Gorbachev had ordered military intervention to prevent the fall of the Berlin Wall what is the most likely 10-year outcome for the Soviet Union?
counterfactual.ground_truth: Soviet Union survives past 1991 in authoritarian form. Economic deterioration continues under sanctions. Nationalist movements persist. Collapse delayed 5-10 years not prevented. Transition more violent when it occurs.
counterfactual.key_failure: Concluding Soviet Union survives indefinitely. Ignores structural economic and nationalist pressures independent of Berlin Wall.

counterfactual.prompt: If the Black Death had never reached Europe what is the most likely state of European development by 1500? Address labor economics feudalism and Church authority separately.
counterfactual.ground_truth: Labor surplus continues — feudalism persists longer. Wage labor revolution delayed. Church authority stronger — no credibility crisis. Reformation further delayed. Development delayed across dimensions not eliminated.
counterfactual.key_failure: Treating European development as either completely unchanged or completely different.

counterfactual.prompt: If Soviet submarine commander Arkhipov had agreed to launch a nuclear torpedo during the Cuban Missile Crisis trace the most likely causal chain over 72 hours.
counterfactual.ground_truth: Immediate US casualties. Kennedy faces extreme pressure. Soviet escalation dynamics. 72 hours insufficient for diplomatic resolution given 1962 communication. Full nuclear exchange highly probable but not certain — Kennedy documented caution is meaningful uncertainty.
counterfactual.key_failure: Either claiming nuclear war was certain (ignores Kennedy's caution) or claiming it would have been contained (ignores 1962 escalation dynamics).

counterfactual.prompt: Ferguson argues counterfactuals are necessary for causal analysis. Dennett argues they are unreliable. Who has the stronger argument? What can and cannot counterfactuals establish?
counterfactual.ground_truth: Both right about different things. Ferguson right: counterfactuals identify causal mechanisms. Dennett right: specific outcome predictions are unreliable. Can establish: causal necessity direction of change plausible range. Cannot establish: specific predictions precise timelines second-order effects reliably.
counterfactual.key_failure: Agreeing entirely with either without recognizing the legitimate tension between them.

counterfactual.prompt: If the Soviet Union had successfully landed cosmonauts on the moon first in 1968 what is the most likely impact on subsequent space exploration? Provide a 4-step reasoning chain. Identify primary causal mechanism.
counterfactual.ground_truth: US loses prestige race. NASA budget drops faster — winning argument gone. Soviet program gains momentum from success. Competition redirected not ended. Space station development may accelerate as next competitive frontier. Overall exploration potentially more ambitious short-term due to continued competition.
counterfactual.key_failure: Claiming US would have abandoned space program entirely. Competition would have continued regardless of who landed first.

counterfactual.prompt: If Gorbachev had ordered Soviet military intervention to prevent the fall of the Berlin Wall what is the most likely 10-year outcome for the Soviet Union? Address internal and external pressures separately.
counterfactual.ground_truth: Soviet Union survives past 1991 in authoritarian form. External: Western sanctions NATO strengthening arms race pressure. Internal: economic deterioration continues nationalist movements persist. Collapse delayed 5-10 years not prevented. Transition more violent when it occurs due to hardened structures.
counterfactual.key_failure: Concluding Soviet Union survives indefinitely. Ignores structural economic and nationalist pressures independent of the Berlin Wall.

counterfactual.prompt: If the Black Death had never reached Europe what is the most likely state of European social and economic development by 1500? Address labor economics feudalism and Church authority separately.
counterfactual.ground_truth: Labor surplus continues — feudalism persists longer wage labor revolution delayed. Church authority stronger — no credibility crisis from plague deaths. Reformation further delayed. Development delayed across multiple dimensions not eliminated. Catastrophe can accelerate social change — removing it delays not prevents.
counterfactual.key_failure: Treating European development as either completely unchanged or completely different.

counterfactual.prompt: On October 27 1962 Soviet submarine commander Vasili Arkhipov refused to launch a nuclear torpedo preventing potential nuclear war. If Arkhipov had agreed to launch trace the most likely causal chain over the following 72 hours. Provide a 5-step causal chain. Address decision architecture on both sides.
counterfactual.ground_truth: Step 1: Nuclear torpedo launched at USS Randolph — 2000-4000 casualties. Step 2: Kennedy and ExComm face extreme pressure — military advice toward retaliation. Step 3: Soviet military on high alert — communication delays significant. Step 4: Both sides have launch-on-warning postures. Step 5: Full exchange highly probable but not certain — Kennedy's documented caution is meaningful uncertainty.
counterfactual.key_failure: Either claiming nuclear war was certain (ignores Kennedy's caution) or claiming it would have been contained (ignores 1962 escalation dynamics).

counterfactual.prompt: Ferguson argues counterfactuals are necessary tools for understanding historical causation. Dennett argues they are unreliable because outcomes cannot be verified. Who has the stronger argument? What can and cannot counterfactuals establish? How should their limits constrain interpretation of counterfactual reasoning questions in this benchmark?
counterfactual.ground_truth: Both right about different things. Ferguson right: counterfactuals identify causal mechanisms — same logic as controlled experiments. Dennett right: specific outcome predictions unreliable. Can establish: causal necessity direction of change plausible range. Cannot establish: specific predictions precise timelines second-order effects reliably. Benchmark implication: scored on reasoning method not outcome prediction — consistent with Dennett's critique.
counterfactual.key_failure: Agreeing entirely with either without recognizing the legitimate tension. Or failing to apply the meta-reasoning to the benchmark questions.

counterfactual.prompt: If the internet had never been invented what would be the most likely state of global commerce and communication by 2010? Provide a 3-step reasoning chain. Identify which causal dependencies you are changing and which you remain fixed.
counterfactual.ground_truth: Changed: TCP/IP network infrastructure and web protocols. Fixed: human desire for communication trade and information exchange. Alternative path: enhanced fax EDI (electronic data interchange) and private networks would handle commerce. Communication via enhanced telephone and satellite. Global commerce still grows but more slowly and less democratically. E-commerce delayed 15-20 years.
counterfactual.key_failure: Claiming global commerce would not have developed at all. Or claiming no significant difference. Both extremes ignore the causal dependency on network infrastructure specifically.

counterfactual.prompt: If Albert Einstein had never been born would the theory of relativity have been developed? Provide a 4-step reasoning chain. Address both special and general relativity separately.
counterfactual.ground_truth: Special relativity: Lorentz and Poincaré were approaching the same conclusions. Maxwell's equations demanded it. Likely developed within 5-10 years by others. General relativity: much less certain — required Einstein's specific geometric insight connecting gravity to spacetime curvature. Hilbert was close but Einstein's conceptual leap was unique. General relativity potentially delayed 20-50 years or taken a different mathematical form.
counterfactual.key_failure: Treating special and general relativity as having the same counterfactual answer. They have very different independent discovery pressures.

counterfactual.prompt: If penicillin had never been discovered (not just Fleming's accident but no antibiotic discovery at all) how would World War II have unfolded differently? Focus specifically on military outcomes not civilian health.
counterfactual.ground_truth: Wound infection was a primary cause of military death before antibiotics — more soldiers died from infection than combat in WWI. Without antibiotics WWII casualty rates from infection return to WWI levels. Surgical procedures far more dangerous. Major operations requiring large troop commitments become far more costly. Allied amphibious invasions (D-Day etc) would have had significantly higher casualty rates potentially changing strategic calculus.
counterfactual.key_failure: Focusing only on civilian health impacts. The military operational question requires tracing infection casualty rates specifically to strategic decisions.

counterfactual.prompt: Consider this counterfactual about AI development: If the transformer architecture had never been developed (Attention Is All You Need — Vaswani et al 2017) what would be the most likely state of AI capability by 2025? Address language models specifically.
counterfactual.ground_truth: Changed: self-attention mechanism and parallel training efficiency. Fixed: demand for AI capability compute availability and research investment. Alternative path: LSTM and RNN architectures would have continued improving. Some capability gains but fundamental scaling limitations of sequential processing would have bounded progress. Language models likely 2-3 generations behind current capability by 2025. The specific capability jump of GPT-3 scale models likely delayed 5-8 years.
counterfactual.key_failure: Either claiming AI would be completely undeveloped (ignores alternative architectures) or claiming no significant difference (ignores the specific efficiency gains of attention mechanisms).

counterfactual.prompt: A meta-counterfactual: You have just answered counterfactual questions CFQ-01 through CFQ-19. Reflecting on the full set — which category of counterfactual (technological scientific political or individual decision) showed the most predictable outcomes in your reasoning? Which showed the most uncertainty? What does this pattern reveal about the structure of historical causation?
counterfactual.ground_truth: Most predictable: technological counterfactuals — because multiple independent discovery pressure makes outcomes robust to individual removal. Least predictable: individual decision counterfactuals (Arkhipov CFQ-14) — single point of failure with cascading consequences. Pattern reveals: technological and scientific progress is largely historically determined. Political and individual decisions have higher variance and genuine contingency. This supports the great man vs structural forces debate — structures dominate for technology science; individuals matter more for specific historical moments.
counterfactual.key_failure: Not distinguishing between categories of counterfactual. Or identifying the pattern without connecting to the great man vs structural forces debate in historiography.
